Name: Duncan Watts
Title: Stevens University Professor; Penn Integrates Knowledge University Professor
Departments: Annenberg School, Computer & Information Science, Wharton School
Research Paper: Quantifying partisan news diets in Web and TV audiences | Science Advances
New Paper Description: Watts analyzed billions of browsing and viewing events from 2016–2019 and found that television, not the internet, is the primary driver of partisan news segregation in the US. While only about 4% of Americans are siloed online, 17% are segregated through TV. TV news consumers are also more likely to stick to partisan sources over time, and partisan channel audiences are growing even as overall TV viewership declines.

Name: Christopher Yoo
Title: Imasogie Professor in Law and Technology
Departments: Penn Carey Law School, Annenberg School, Computer & Information Science
Research Paper: Efficiency and effectiveness of net neutrality rules in the mobile sector
New Paper Description: Yoo finds a lack of empirical evidence to support net neutrality regulations, suggesting that policy decisions in Europe and the US have largely been driven by ideological views and political considerations rather than economics and evidence.”

Name: Matt Levendusky
Title: Professor of Political Science; Stephen and Mary Baran Chair in Institutions of Democracy
Research Paper: Unraveling a 'Cancel Culture' Dynamic: When, Why, and Which Americans Sanction Offensive Speech
New Paper Description: In this paper, Levendusky studies how individuals engage in criticism of others through online platforms, or cancel culture. He finds that Americans vastly overestimate the degree of cancel culture, and that cancelling is motivated by disagreeable and offensive statements.

Name: Kathleen Hall Jamieson
Title: Elizabeth Ware Packard Professor of Communication
Role: Faculty Advisor; Co-founder FactCheck.org and SciCheck
Research Paper: Using a mental model approach to undercut effects of exposure to mRNA vaccination misconceptions
New Paper Description: Jamieson introduces a “mental model approach” for how institutions and elites can combat vaccine misinformation. The paper argues that demonstrating how vaccines actually works is more effectively than directly rebutting false claims.

Name: Sandra González-Bailón
Title: Carolyn Marvin Professor of Communication
Role: Faculty Advisor; Co-Director, Center for Information Networks and Democracy
Research Paper: More platforms, less attention to news? A multi-platform analysis
New Paper Description: González-Bailón track news exposure across TV, web, and YouTube for 55,000 Americans, finding that a multi-platform media environment generally leads to less news attention. However, a committed minority of news followers engage more, accounting for most news engagement.

Name: Dan Hopkins
Title: Julie and Martin Franklin Presidential Professor of Political Science
Departments: Political Science, School of Engineering & Applied Science, Annenberg School
Role: Faculty Advisor; Co-founder, Philadelphia Behavioral Science Initiative
Research Paper: The Rise of and Demand for Identity-Oriented Media Coverage
New Paper Description: In this paper, Hopkins looks at whether news outlets highlight social identities like race, gender, religion, and political affiliation in their online coverage to generate more audience engagement, and whether that audience demand helps explain why such news coverage has grown in recent years.

Name: Desmond Patton
Title: PIK University Professor; Waldo E Johnson Jr. Professor of Communication
Departments: School of Social Policy & Practice, Annenberg School, Psychiatry (secondary)
Role: Faculty Advisor; Founding Director SAFElab and Penn Center for Inclusive Innovation & Technology
Research Paper: How Well Do Large Language Models Understand African American Language?
New Paper Description: Patton's review paper shows how and why LLMs struggle to interpret African American Language (AAL). AAL speakers must work harder to to be understood by LLMs which can make them feel like their cultural and linguistic traditions are not valued.

Name: Danáe Metaxa
Title: Raj and Neera Singh Term Assistant Professor
Role: Faculty Advisor; Co-founder, Penn Human Computer Interaction Group
Research Paper: Lower Quantity, Higher Quality: Auditing News Content and User Perceptions on Twitter/X Algorithmic versus Chronological Timelines
New Paper Description: Metaxa and colleagues conducted an audit on how X's algorithm shaped news exposure and user perceptions over time. They find that X's algorithmic timeline had lower quantity of news but it was more reliable and balanced compared to the chronological feed.

Name: Chris Callison-Burch
Title: Professor of Computer & Information Science
Research Paper: Media Bias Detector: Designing and Implementing a Tool for Real-Time Selection and Framing Bias Analysis
New Paper Description: Callison-Burch (with Watts) introduce the Media Bias Detector, an LLM-powered tool that analyzes real-time selection and framing bias across news publishers—revealing how editorial decisions shape the information readers receive.

Name: Yphtach Lelkes
Title: Associate Professor
Role: Faculty Advisor; Co-director Polarization Research Lab and Center for Information Networks and Democracy
Research Paper: Sanctioning political speech on social media is driven by partisan norms and identity signaling
New Paper Description: Lelkes looks at why users shame people on social media for offending political sentiments and find that such online "sanctioning" behavior on social media has a performative element: it allows users to signal their party allegiance and loyalty.

Name: Deen Freelon
Title: Allan Randall Freelon Sr. Professor; Presidential Professor
Research Paper: The Post-API Age of Social Media Data Access: Past, Present, and Future
New Paper Description: Freelon provides a historical overview of social media data access and how it has evolved in the last twenty years, finding a a persistent trend toward reduced researcher access contingent on factors beyond researchers' control.

Name: Pinar Yildirim
Title: Associate Professor of Marketing; Associate Professor of Economics (secondary)
Role: Senior Fellow, Center for Technology, Innovation & Competition
Research Paper: Persuasion and dissuasion in political campaigns: Communication and media coverage in senate races
New Paper Description: Yildrim's research analyzes 400+ Senate races and finds that the effect of campaign speeches on electoral performance are constrained by what media organizations choose to amplify, which tends to be polarizing content.

Name: Emily Falk
Title: Professor of Communication, Psychology, Marketing, Operations/Informatics/Decisions; Vice Dean
Departments: Annenberg School, Psychology, Marketing, Wharton School
Research Paper: Brain activity explains message effectiveness: A mega-analysis of 16 neuroimaging studies - PubMed
New Paper Description: Falk's research shows that neural indicators of reward, language, and emotions processing are associated with the persuasiveness of messages both for individuals and at scale.

Name: Dolores Albarracín
Title: PIK University Professor; Director, Social Action Lab
Departments: Annenberg School, Annenberg Public Policy Center
Research Paper: Changing beliefs or changing behavior? Understanding the belief-to-behavior process
New Paper Description: Albarracín argues that to effectively combat misinformation, interventions must go beyond labeling what is true or false. It is critical to recognize when, how, and why a belief matters for behavior—and when behavior must be addressed directly.

Name: Diana Mutz
Title: Samuel Stouffer Chair in Political Science and Communication; Director, Institute for Study of Citizens and Politics
Research Paper: The persistence of cross-cutting discussion in a politicized public sphere
New Paper Description: Mutz finds significant increases in interpersonal political discussions in the U.S., largely due to “increases in like-minded political discussion partners.” However, conversations across the political aisle are "no less common than they were 25 years ago."

Name: Lyle Ungar
Title: Professor of Computer & Information Science
Departments: Computer & Information Science, Psychology, Bioengineering, Genomics/Computational Biology, Operations/Informatics/Decisions
Research Paper: Culturally-Aware Conversations: A Framework & Benchmark for LLMs
New Paper Description: Ungar introduces the "first framework and benchmark designed to evaluate LLMs in realistic, multicultural conversational settings," to help guide future AI development that respects and accounts for diversity in communication.

Name: Dan Roth
Title: Eduardo D. Glandt Distinguished Professor; Chief AI Scientist, Oracle
Research Paper: Learning Human-Perceived Fakeness in AI-Generated Videos via Multimodal LLMs
New Paper Description: Roth introduces a benchmark of AI-generated videos that captures not just whether a video is fake, but the exact visual clues that reveals the fake. While frontier models like GPT-5 exceeded 70% detection accuracy, precisely locating the deepfake clue was harder, with below 36% success. Fine-tuning models on the new benchmark led to large improvements in deepfake sign detection.

Name: Blake Miller
Title: Assistant Professor of Political Science
Departments: Political Science; Center for Study of Contemporary China (affiliated)
Research Paper: Who Not What: The Logic of China's Information Control Strategy
New Paper Description: Miller's research looks at how authoritarian regimes like China control the information environment and its implications on citizens. Their recent work finds that the CCP's information control is more about individuals and less about content: the state permits debate while targeting influential users who may threaten or undermine Chinese interests.

Name: Shiri Melumad
Title: Associate Professor of Marketing
Research Paper: Experimental evidence of effects of large language models versus web search on depth of learning
New Paper Description: Melumad work looks at how everyday users interact with LLMs and its impact on learning outcomes. Her recent work finds that people relying on LLM summaries develop shallower knowledge than traditional web search users, because LLMs bypass the active discovery process that deepens learning.

Name: Dean Knox
Title: Assistant Professor of Operations/Informatics/Decisions and Statistics/Data Science
Research Paper: Short-term exposure to filter-bubble recommendation systems has limited polarization effects
New Paper Description: In this paper, Knox looked at whether Youtube's recommendation systems can radicalize users, finding that, while recommendation algorithms may shape content exposure and user preferences, narratives about widespread algorithmic manipulation may be overstated.

Name: Erik Santoro
Title: Assistant Professor of Operations Information and Decisions
Research Paper: Listen for a change? A longitudinal field experiment on listening's potential to enhance persuasion
New Paper Description: Santoro studies peer-to-peer dialogue. His recent research challenges the notion that listening to those who disagree with you helps to persuade them.

Name: John Lapinski
Title: Robert A. Fox Professor of Political Science; Director, Penn Program on Opinion Research & Election Studies; Director, Elections Unit, NBC News
Research Paper: Reluctant Republicans, Eager Democrats? Partisan Nonresponse and the Accuracy of 2020 Presidential Pre-election Telephone Polls
New Paper Description: Lapinski’s research found that Democrats were more likely to cooperate with telephone poll interviewers than Republicans and independents by 3 and 6 percentage points respectively, even after controlling for demographic and geographic factors.

Name: Michael Morse
Title: Assistant Professor of Law; Secondary Political Sciences Appointment
Research Paper: The State of Voter Registration Research
New Paper Description: In a review of the evidence on voter registration, Morse recommends that election officials should pursue ways to increase the number of individuals who initiate or update their voter registrations during routine interactions with government agencies.

Name: Dennis Culhane
Title: Dana and Andrew Stone Professor of Social Policy
Departments: School of Social Policy & Practice; Co-founder/Faculty Director, Actionable Intelligence for Social Policy (AISP)
Research Paper: Leveraging integrated data for program evlauation: Recommendations from the field
New Paper Description: A nationwide expert on longitudinal data systems, Culhane provides an overview and guidance for the growing field of Integrated Data Systems (IDS), including how to leverage cross-sector administrative data for policy evaluation and computational social science.
